title: How to Set Up Your First Professional LinkedIn Profile as a Student
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You're a student. You don't have a decade of work experience. You haven't led a sales team or managed a product launch. So what exactly are you supposed to put on a LinkedIn profile? More than you think. Recruiters looking for interns and entry-level candidates are not searching for industry veterans. They're looking for potential. Here is how to build a LinkedIn profile that shows yours, even when your resume is still taking shape.

Why LinkedIn Matters Now

LinkedIn is not a platform you join after you have a career. It's a platform you use to build one. Recruiters search LinkedIn for candidates by skills, education, and location. If you don't have a profile, you're invisible to those searches. If your profile is incomplete, you look like you're not serious.

Your LinkedIn profile is also the first result when someone Googles your name. That's true whether you build it or not. If you leave it empty, the empty profile is your first impression. If you build it thoughtfully, you control what people find.

The Profile Photo and Banner

You need a photo. Profiles with photos receive far more connection requests and messages than profiles without them. The photo doesn't need to be a professional headshot, though those are nice if you can get one. It needs to be a clear photo of your face, alone, in decent lighting, wearing something you'd wear to a job interview. No group shots where someone's arm is around your shoulder and cropped out. No photos from your cousin's wedding where you're holding a glass of champagne.

The background banner is optional but useful. A photo of your campus, a simple geometric pattern, or a relevant image related to your field adds visual polish. It signals that you put effort into the profile. Small details matter when you're competing for attention.

Your Headline Is Not Your Job Title

The headline is the line of text directly under your name. LinkedIn defaults to your current job title or your major. Do not let it. Your headline is searchable. It should include keywords a recruiter might use to find someone like you.

Instead of "Student at University of Michigan," try "Computer Science Student | Aspiring Software Engineer | Python and Java." Instead of "Marketing Major," try "Marketing Student | Social Media and Content Creation | Seeking Summer Internship."

You are telling LinkedIn's algorithm who to show your profile to. A recruiter searching for "social media intern" will find the second headline. They will not find the first.

The About Section

The About section is your opportunity to tell a story that your resume can't. Write three to four short paragraphs. Start with who you are and what you're studying. Follow with what you're interested in and what problems you want to solve. End with what you're looking for next, whether that's an internship, a research opportunity, or networking connections.

Write in first person. Keep it professional but not stiff. Your goal is to sound like a motivated person someone would want to work with. Do not write "Results-oriented undergraduate pursuing opportunities in dynamic environments." Nobody talks like that. Write "I'm a sophomore studying environmental science. I'm interested in water quality policy and spent last summer volunteering with a river conservation project. I'm currently looking for a research assistant position where I can build field experience."

Your Experience Section

You might not have formal work experience. That's okay. The experience section is for anything you did that demonstrates skills. Summer jobs. Volunteer work. Club leadership. Tutoring. Campus organizations. A part-time job at a coffee shop shows customer service and reliability. Running social media for a student club shows content creation and project management.

Describe each experience using action verbs and specific details. Instead of "Helped with events," write "Coordinated three campus events per semester, managing logistics for audiences of 50 to 100 students." Instead of "Worked as a cashier," write "Processed 100+ daily transactions while maintaining accurate cash drawer balancing." Be concrete. Be specific. Show what you actually did.

The Education Section

List your university, your expected graduation year, and your major and minor. Add relevant coursework if it aligns with the roles you're targeting. A biology student seeking lab internships might list "Genetics, Organic Chemistry, and Biostatistics." A marketing student might list "Consumer Behavior, Digital Marketing, and Data Analytics."

Include your GPA if it's above a 3.5. If it's below that, leave it off. No one will assume a bad GPA because you didn't list one. They'll just assume you chose not to include it.

Skills and Endorsements

Add skills that are relevant to your field. Technical skills like Excel, Python, Photoshop, or SPSS. Soft skills like public speaking, leadership, or teamwork. LinkedIn allows up to 50 skills. You don't need 50. List 10 to 15 that accurately represent what you can do.

Ask classmates, professors, or club advisors to endorse your skills. Endorsements are not deeply meaningful in a hiring context, but they add legitimacy to your profile. A skill with zero endorsements looks like you listed it because you once opened the program. A skill with several endorsements looks like other people can vouch for it.

Start Connecting

Connect with classmates. Connect with professors. Connect with guest speakers from your classes. Send a short note with each request reminding them how you know each other. "Hi Professor Chen, I'm in your Tuesday macroeconomics class. I'd like to connect on LinkedIn." Personal notes increase acceptance rates significantly.

Follow companies you're interested in. Comment thoughtfully on posts in your field. LinkedIn is a professional network, not a passive resume. The more you engage, the more visible your profile becomes.

The Bottom Line

You don't need work experience to build a strong LinkedIn profile. You need a clear photo, a keyword-rich headline, a story in your About section, and experience descriptions that highlight what you've done so far, even if that work was unpaid, part-time, or extracurricular. Start now. Add to it over time. By the time you're ready to apply for internships and jobs, your profile will already be working for you.
